title: Input & Output
beginnerExplanation: Input lets your program receive data from users. Output (print) displays results. Together, they make programs interactive.
intermediateExplanation: A game that never asks the player anything or shows anything is useless. I/O is how your program communicates with the outside world.

realWorldAnalogy.title: A Conversation
realWorldAnalogy.description: Think of I/O as a conversation. input() is you asking a question and waiting for a reply. print() is you speaking the answer. Without both, there is no dialogue.

mentalModel: Think of I/O as a conversation. input() is you asking a question and waiting for a reply. print() is you speaking the answer. Without both, there is no dialogue.

graph TD

  Process --> End
executionWalkthrough: 2. Scopes and local namespaces are verified.
3. Target variables are allocated or updated.
commonMistakes: Forgetting that input() returns a string, leading to "55" instead of 10 when adding.
Not providing a prompt, leaving the user staring at a blank screen.
Mixing up print() and return — print shows text, return sends a value.

debuggingMission.scenario: An adventurer is trying to use Input & Output but hit a common pitfall: Forgetting that input() returns a string, leading to "55" instead of 10 when adding.
debuggingMission.explanation: Make sure to follow the best practices: Always provide a clear prompt message inside input().

practiceQuestions.question: What type does input() always return?
options: It depends on what the user types
practiceQuestions.explanation: input() always returns a string, even if the user types a number. You must convert it explicitly.

challengeQuestions.question: What is the correct keyword or operator most commonly associated with Input & Output?
hints: Look at the syntax summary of the topic.
Check standard keyword usage in Python.

projectExercise.title: Mastering Input & Output
projectExercise.description: Implement a clean snippet demonstrating the use of Input & Output.
requirements: Write a snippet utilizing the syntax: name = input("prompt")\nprint(value)

retentionSystem.revision1: Define the core syntax of Input & Output.
retentionSystem.revision2: Name one common mistake when using Input & Output.

developerMode.industryUsage: Input & Output is widely used in standard library scripts and enterprise Python programs.
developerMode.performanceConsiderations: Optimized by standard compiler rules in modern Python environments.
bestPractices: Always provide a clear prompt message inside input().
Remember that input() always returns a string — convert with int() or float() if you need a number.
Use f-strings for clean, readable output: print(f"Score: {score}").
antiPatterns: Forgetting that input() returns a string, leading to "55" instead of 10 when adding.
Not providing a prompt, leaving the user staring at a blank screen.
Mixing up print() and return — print shows text, return sends a value.
developerMode.architectureNotes: Interpreted and executed in the primary application call loop.
